content:
## What Is Split Tunneling?

With a standard VPN, all your internet traffic goes through the encrypted tunnel. Split tunneling changes that: you decide which apps or websites use the VPN and which connect directly to the internet through your regular ISP connection.

## Why It Matters

The main reason to use split tunneling is speed and convenience. Streaming local content, using a local banking app, or accessing your home network printer works better without routing through a VPN server in another country. Meanwhile, your sensitive browsing or work traffic stays protected.

## Two Common Approaches

App-based split tunneling lets you select specific applications to include or exclude from the VPN tunnel. For example, you might route your browser and email client through the VPN but let Spotify connect directly.

URL-based split tunneling (sometimes called inverse split tunneling) lets you specify websites rather than apps. Some VPN clients support both modes.

## Security Trade-Offs

Split tunneling reduces your overall privacy since part of your traffic bypasses the VPN. DNS leaks become more likely if not configured carefully. If your threat model requires full traffic encryption, disable split tunneling entirely.

## Which VPNs Support It

ExpressVPN, NordVPN, and Surfshark all offer split tunneling on Windows, Mac, and Android. iOS support is more limited due to Apple's VPN API restrictions. Always check your VPN client's settings panel under Advanced or Preferences.
description: Split tunneling lets you choose which apps go through your VPN and which use your regular connection. Here's how it works and when to use it.
title: VPN Split Tunneling Explained: Route Only the Traffic You Want
